3:I[22016,["/_next/static/chunks/0ck96axwq11f0.js","/_next/static/chunks/0d3shmwh5_nmn.js","/_next/static/chunks/0vkb6e1f~sbaj.js"],""]
9:I[97367,["/_next/static/chunks/0ck96axwq11f0.js","/_next/static/chunks/0d3shmwh5_nmn.js"],"OutletBoundary"]
a:"$Sreact.suspense"
4:T1113,<p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="0">
There is a photograph of Christophe Lemaire from the early 1990s. He is wearing a white shirt, dark trousers, and a watch with a plain leather strap. Thirty years later, he is wearing essentially the same thing. This is not a lack of imagination. It is a clarity of vision.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="1">"I am not interested in novelty for its own sake," Lemaire told me when we spoke in his Paris atelier. "I am interested in the evolution of an idea." </p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="2">The Elimination</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="3">Walk into a Lemaire store—whether in Paris, Tokyo, or Seoul—and you notice the space before you notice the clothes. High ceilings. Natural light. Stone floors that have been walked for centuries. The garments hang simply, spaced apart, each given room to breathe.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="4">This is not accidental. It is the physical manifestation of a design philosophy.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="5">"Each season, we start with too much," Lemaire explains. "And then we eliminate. What can be removed without losing the essence? The button that is only decorative. The seam that serves no structural purpose. The color that draws attention to itself rather than the whole."</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="6">This process of elimination is Daoist. It is 为道日损 (wei dao ri sun)—"in the pursuit of Dao, every day something is dropped."</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="7">The Anti-Logo</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="8">Lemaire's garments carry no external branding. No logo on the chest. No signature hardware. The only identifier is internal—a small tag with the season code, meant to be seen only by the wearer when dressing.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="9">"The confidence of a garment should be in its cut, its material, its proportion," Lemaire says. "Not in a name that tells others what you paid for it."</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="10">This is radical in an age of conspicuous consumption. The Lemaire customer is not buying status. They are buying something closer to privacy. The luxury of being unremarkable, of passing through the world without being read.</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="11">The Wearing</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="12">Lemaire's clothes are designed to change. The cotton softens. The silk develops a patina. The colors—mostly drawn from nature: charcoal, sand, olive, ink—shift subtly with washing and wearing.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="13">"We design for the fifth wearing, the tenth wearing, the hundredth wearing," Lemaire says. "The garment should become more itself over time, not less."</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="14">This is 反璞归真 (fan pu gui zhen)—returning to the uncarved block. The garment that becomes more authentic as it loses its newness.</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="15">The Future of Less</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="16">As we spoke, Lemaire was preparing his Spring/Summer 2027 collection. He showed me a swatch of fabric—cotton mixed with silk, undyed, with visible flecks of the plant matter it was grown from.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="17">"This is where we are going," he said. "Not back to nature in a romantic sense. Forward to a new honesty about what things are and how they are made."</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="18">The art of doing less, it turns out, requires more skill than the art of doing more. Every decision carries weight when there are fewer of them. Every proportion matters when there is no ornament to distract from it.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="19">Lemaire's work is a masterclass in this economy of means. It is fashion as meditation. Dressing as a form of self-respect. The quiet confidence of someone who has nothing to prove.</p><hr class="border-t border-[#e5e5e5] my-12" key="20" /><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="21">*Interview conducted in Paris, March 2026. Edited for clarity.*
    </p>0:{"rsc":["$","$1","c",{"children":[["$","main",null,{"className":"min-h-screen","children":[["$","$L2",null,{}],["$","article",null,{"className":"pt-32 pb-20 px-6","children":["$","div",null,{"className":"max-w-3xl mx-auto","children":[["$","$L3",null,{"href":"/","className":"text-sm tracking-widest uppercase text-[#8a8a8a] hover:text-[#1a1a1a] transition-colors mb-12 inline-block","children":"← Back"}],["$","header",null,{"className":"mb-16","children":[["$","div",null,{"className":"flex items-center space-x-4 mb-6","children":[["$","span",null,{"className":"text-xs tracking-[0.2em] uppercase text-[#c9a961]","children":"essence"}],["$","span",null,{"className":"text-xs text-[#8a8a8a]","children":["2026.03.20"," · ",12," min read"]}]]}],["$","h1",null,{"className":"text-4xl md:text-5xl font-light leading-tight mb-8","children":"Lemaire: The Art of Doing Less"}],["$","p",null,{"className":"text-xl text-[#8a8a8a] leading-relaxed max-w-2xl","children":"Christophe Lemaire on intentionality, elimination, and the quiet confidence of clothes that don't try too hard."}]]}],["$","div",null,{"className":"w-px h-16 bg-gradient-to-b from-[#1a1a1a] to-transparent mx-auto mb-16"}],["$","div",null,{"className":"prose prose-lg max-w-none","dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$4"}}],"$L5"]}]}],"$L6"]}],["$L7"],"$L8"]}],"isPartial":false,"staleTime":300,"varyParams":null,"buildId":"o7DrkifCy5v8ywH61CyMI"}
